fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to ship the ordered list positioning engine we've been talking about. The core idea is that any record type should be able to 'join' an ordered list and have all the bookkeeping done automatically — no more manual position juggling when things get inserted, moved around, or deleted. We've had bugs in the past where gaps never closed after deletions, or positions collided when someone inserted in the middle, and that's exactly what this should fix.

A few things I know we need: records should default to going to the end of the list when created, but we need a way to flip that so new stuff shows up at the top instead. There should also be a way to completely opt out of auto-placement. We talked about supporting sub-lists (like, the same table can have multiple independent ordered lists based on some grouping field). Oh, and remember that constraint thing we ran into on the login module — same deal applies here, the shifting logic needs to handle that edge case gracefully.

Also, someone on the data team asked about zero-based ordering for one of their exports, so that config knob needs to be in there. And we definitely need timestamp behavior to be controllable — sometimes you don't want a reorder to look like a content edit. There's also a bulk-operation mode where you want to pause the auto-maintenance temporarily. Error handling should be clean and categorized, no raw exceptions leaking out.

trigger_keywords: where does a new record go by default
default placement for created records
what happens when add_new_at is not specified
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: By default, when no placement config is specified, new records are appended to the end (bottom) of their list and receive the next sequential position. The default value for add_new_at is 'bottom'. If you set it to 'top', new records are prepended. If you set it to null, records are created with no position and do not participate in the ordering at all.

trigger_keywords: what number does the list start at
first position value
top_of_list default value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: By default the list starts numbering at 1 (top_of_list defaults to 1). This is configurable: setting top_of_list to 0 makes the list behave like a zero-indexed array, so the first element gets position 0, the second gets 1, and so on. All position-based operations (insert_at, move_to_top, etc.) use the same configured scale.

trigger_keywords: unique constraint edge case
constraint thing from login module
sequential updates unique index collision
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the position column is backed by a unique database constraint (configured via unique: true), the library automatically switches to stepwise (sequential) shifting mode. Instead of bulk-updating all affected rows at once — which would transiently produce duplicate position values and violate the unique index — it shifts each intermediate element one at a time. This mode is called sequential_updates (or stepwise_shuffle in query output). It turns on automatically when unique is true, but can be explicitly overridden by setting sequential_updates: false in config.

trigger_keywords: how do sub-lists work
partitioning records into independent lists
scope key for grouped ordering
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Sub-lists are defined using the scope config key. You provide one field name (e.g., scope: 'parent_id') or a list of field names (e.g., scope: ['org_id', 'board_id']). Records that share the same value(s) for all scope key(s) form one independent ordered list with its own contiguous numbering. Records in a different scope bucket form a completely separate list. Moving a record to a different scope (by updating its scope key) removes it from the old list (closing the gap) and inserts it into the new list, either at the end or at an explicit position.

trigger_keywords: pause auto maintenance
bulk operation mode
suspend list bookkeeping temporarily
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You can suspend automatic list maintenance for a block of operations using the suspend_maintenance operation, which wraps a nested list of operations. While suspended, position changes and deletions are persisted as raw writes without triggering the usual gap-closing or collision-shifting logic. This is intended for bulk operations where the caller will fix up positions manually. The suspend_maintenance facility also accepts an optional argument naming additional record types to suspend, but that argument must be an array and every element must be a valid persistable record class — supplying anything else is rejected up front with a categorized error (argument_not_array or argument_not_record_classes).

trigger_keywords: timestamp behavior on reorder
does moving a record update modified_at
touch_on_update config
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: By default, when a reordering operation changes a record's position, the modified-timestamp of every record whose position actually changed is refreshed to the current time (touch_on_update defaults to true). Records that did not move keep their earlier timestamp. This can be disabled by setting touch_on_update: false in config, in which case all timestamps are left untouched even though positions still update correctly. The at_time operation in the test harness simulates time so timestamp behavior can be verified.

trigger_keywords: remove vs delete difference
what happens when you remove a record
does deleting shift trailing elements
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: remove takes a record out of the ordering without destroying it: its position is cleared (becomes nil/empty), it no longer participates in the list, and every element that was behind it shifts one place toward the front to close the gap. delete destroys the record entirely and also closes the gap for trailing elements. Importantly, if you remove a record first and then delete it, the trailing elements are NOT shifted a second time — the gap was already closed by the remove.

trigger_keywords: insert at position for existing record
move record to absolute slot
set_position vs insert_at difference
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: There are two ways to place an existing in-list record at an absolute position. insert_at relocates the record to a target slot and shifts the elements between the old and new position by one to absorb the move (moving toward the back shifts intervening elements toward the front; moving toward the front shifts them toward the back). set_position directly assigns a new position value and persists it, with equivalent reordering effect. Both produce a contiguous, duplicate-free list afterward. Note: a relocation target below the configured top_of_list is rejected with a categorized error when the positive constraint is active.

trigger_keywords: predecessor and successor queries
neighbors of a record
predecessors and successors with limit
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You can query the immediate predecessor (element one place toward the front) or immediate successor (element one place toward the back) of any record. The head of the list has no predecessor (returns nil) and the tail has no successor (returns nil). You can also request the full ordered group of all predecessors or all successors (nearest first), with an optional limit to cap how many are returned. An element not in the list has no neighbors.

trigger_keywords: error output format
how are errors reported
normalized error categories in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All errors are reported as neutral, language-independent categorized strings in the format error=<category>. No host-language exception names, stack traces, or runtime artifacts appear in stdout. Known error categories include argument_not_array (suspend_maintenance argument is not an array) and argument_not_record_classes (array contains something that is not a persistable record type). The insert_at_outcome and suspend_argument_outcome query types in the test harness are specifically designed to capture and normalize these error outputs.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that constraint thing we ran into on the login module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The sequential_updates / stepwise_shuffle behavior triggered by unique: true in config — when a unique index backs the position column, shifts must happen one row at a time to avoid transient uniqueness violations. See feature17_sequential_updates.json and the sequential_updates / unique / positive config knobs.

context_pointers.vague_reference: timestamp behavior to be controllable — sometimes you don't want a reorder to look like a content edit
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The touch_on_update config boolean (default true). When false, reordering operations do not refresh modified-timestamps on shifted records. Covered in feature18_touch_timestamps.json using the at_time operation wrapper and timestamp_buckets query.

The scope config key, which accepts either a single string field name or an array of field names. Records are partitioned into independent sub-lists based on matching values for all scope key(s). Covered in feature13_scoped_lists.json and feature14_move_across_scope.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: bulk-operation mode where you want to pause the auto-maintenance temporarily
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The suspend_maintenance operation, which accepts a nested operations array and an optional argument for additional record types to suspend. Validation of the argument is strict — must be an array of record classes. Covered in feature15_suspend_maintenance.json and feature16_suspend_argument_errors.json.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a great question, but I don't have specific details on that aspect of the requirement. I'd recommend checking the existing test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the relevant feature, or looking at the configuration options documented in start.md — most behavioral details are captured there. If it's still unclear, let's sync and I can try to clarify the business intent.

feature10_set_position.json.description: Assigning an element a new absolute position by setting its position value directly (and persisting it) relocates the element and reorders the others so the list stays contiguous with no duplicates. The effect matches relocating the element to that target position: elements between the old and new slot shift by one to absorb the change. Each case uses an unscoped (single, global) list of four elements numbered one through four (identifiers one through four) and sets one element's position, then reads the resulting identifier order.

feature11_remove_from_list.json.description: Taking an element out of the ordering clears its position (it becomes empty) and shifts every element that was behind it one place toward the front so the remaining list is contiguous; the removed element reports as no longer in the list. Removing an element and then deleting that same record does not shift the trailing elements a second time. Each case starts from a four-element list numbered one through four (identifiers one through four).

feature12_delete_closes_gap.json.description: Deleting a record that is part of the list automatically closes the gap: every element that was behind the deleted one moves up by a single position so the remaining elements stay contiguously numbered from the top. Deleting more than one element compounds the effect. Each case starts from a four-element list numbered one through four (identifiers one through four).

feature13_scoped_lists.json.description: A list can be partitioned into independent sub-lists by one or more scope keys, so that positions are tracked separately within each scope. Records sharing the same scope key value(s) form one ordered list numbered from the top, and records in a different scope form a wholly separate list with its own independent numbering. The scope can be a single key or a combination of several keys (a record belongs to the same sub-list only when all scope key values match).

feature14_move_across_scope.json.description: When a record's scope key changes, it leaves its old sub-list and joins the target sub-list. The old sub-list closes the gap left behind so it stays contiguous, and the record takes a position in the new sub-list. Changing the scope key together with an explicit position inserts the record at that position in the destination list, shifting the destination's existing elements; moving a record to another scope without specifying a position appends it to the end of the destination list.

feature15_suspend_maintenance.json.description: List maintenance can be suspended for the duration of a block of operations, so that the automatic position adjustments that normally keep the list contiguous are skipped while still persisting the raw changes. Within a suspended block, updating one element's position to collide with another does not shuffle the others, and deleting an element does not pull the trailing elements up. This is used for bulk operations where the caller will fix up positions itself.

feature16_suspend_argument_errors.json.description: The suspend-maintenance facility accepts an optional first argument naming additional record types whose list maintenance should also be suspended. This argument is validated: it must be a collection, and every entry must be a persistable record type. Supplying a value that is not a collection, or a collection containing something that is not a record type, is rejected up front with a neutral, categorized error rather than proceeding. The outcome is reported as a normalized error category.

feature17_sequential_updates.json.description: When the position column is backed by a unique constraint, the library shifts intermediate elements one at a time (stepwise) during a relocation instead of in a single bulk update, so the unique constraint is never transiently violated; this stepwise mode turns on automatically in the presence of a unique index and can be explicitly overridden off. Relocations still produce a contiguous, duplicate-free ordering. A relocation target below the top of the list is rejected with a neutral error. Reports cover whether stepwise shuffling is active, the ordering after a relocation, and the rejection of an out-of-range target.

feature18_touch_timestamps.json.description: By default, when a reordering operation changes positions, the modified-timestamp of every record whose position actually changed is refreshed to the current time, while records that did not move keep their earlier timestamp. This timestamp touching can be disabled by configuration, in which case a reordering (including the gap-closing after a deletion) leaves all timestamps untouched even though positions still update. Each case first creates four elements at a baseline time, then performs one operation at a later time; the report buckets each remaining element's modified-timestamp as either the baseline or the later moment.

feature1_add_at_bottom.json.description: By default a new record is appended to the end of its list and receives the next sequential position, with the list numbered starting at one. Creating several records into the same empty list yields strictly increasing positions in creation order. For any record the status reports its position, whether it is the first element of the list, whether it is the last element, and whether it currently participates in the ordering. A record is 'first' when no element precedes it and 'last' when none follows it.

cases.expected_output:
position=1 first=true last=false in_list=true
position=2 first=false last=false in_list=true
position=3 first=false last=true in_list=true
ids=[1, 2, 3]

feature2_add_at_top.json.description: A list can be configured so that new records are prepended to the front instead of appended to the back. Under this configuration, each newly created record (when no explicit position is supplied) takes the top position and pushes the existing elements one place toward the back, so the most recently created record appears first when the list is read in position order. If, however, an explicit position value is supplied at creation time, that supplied value is honored rather than the automatic top placement.

feature3_no_auto_add.json.description: A list can be configured so that newly created records are NOT automatically placed into the ordering. Under this configuration a created record has no position assigned (its position is empty) and it does not participate in the list, so it reports as not being in the list. A subsequent update of an unrelated attribute on such a record does not draw it into the ordering either: its position remains empty and it remains outside the list.

cases.expected_output:
position=nil first=false last=false in_list=false
position=nil first=false last=false in_list=false

feature4_zero_based_indexing.json.description: The integer used for the top of the list is configurable; when it is set to zero, the list behaves like a zero-indexed array. New records appended to an empty list then start at position zero and increase by one, and the first element of the list reports position zero. Repositioning an element by an absolute target position uses the same zero-based scale.

cases.expected_output:
position=0 first=true last=false in_list=true
position=2 first=false last=true in_list=true
ids=[1, 2, 3]
positions=[0, 1, 2]

feature5_create_at_position.json.description: When a record is created with an explicit position value rather than letting it default to the end, the record is inserted at that exact position and every existing element at or below that position is shifted one place toward the back to make room. The list remains contiguously numbered after the insertion, and reading it in position order shows the new record occupying the requested slot.

feature6_relationship_queries.json.description: For any element in the list you can ask for its immediate predecessor (the element one place ahead of it) and its immediate successor (the element one place behind it). The head of the list has no predecessor and the tail has no successor. You can also ask whether an element is currently in the list and what its current position is; an element that has been taken out of the list reports no position and is no longer first, last, or in the list.

feature7_neighbor_groups.json.description: Beyond a single neighbor, you can request the ordered group of all elements that follow a given element (its successors, nearest first) or all elements that precede it (its predecessors, nearest first). An optional limit caps how many are returned. The head of the list has no predecessors and the tail has no successors. Predecessors are returned in order of increasing distance going toward the front, and successors in order of increasing distance going toward the back.

cases.expected_output:
successors=[2, 3, 4]
successors=[4]
successors=[2, 3]
successors=[]
predecessors=[2, 1]
predecessors=[1]
predecessors=[3, 2]
predecessors=[]

feature8_relative_moves.json.description: An element can be moved relative to its neighbors or to the ends of the list while the remaining elements close ranks to keep the numbering contiguous. Moving an element one step toward the back swaps it with its immediate successor; moving it one step toward the front swaps it with its immediate predecessor. Moving an element to the very front or the very back relocates it there and shifts the intervening elements accordingly. Each case sets up a four-element list numbered one through four (identifiers also one through four in that order) and performs a single move, then reads the resulting order of identifiers.

feature9_insert_at.json.description: An element already in the list can be relocated to an absolute target position. The intervening elements between the element's old and new position shift by one place to absorb the move, and the list stays contiguously numbered. Moving toward the back shifts the elements in between one place toward the front; moving toward the front shifts them one place toward the back. Each case starts from a four-element list numbered one through four (identifiers one through four in that order) and relocates one element, then reads the resulting identifier order and position values.
